meta.topic: Need(n't), don't need to and don't have to

step_1_context.text: We can use need as an ordinary verb:

step_2_explanation.simple_rule:
We can use need as an ordinary verb:
 • I needed to leave early.
step_2_explanation.formula:
To say it is not necessary to do something, we can use either needn't or don't need to:
 • You needn't cut the grass, I'll do it later.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake:
We can often use either needn't or don't have to with little difference in meaning to say that it is unnecessary to do something:
 • You needn't whisper.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 19.1.1 You don't need to / needn't worry about the exam. You've prepared really well.
options: Both don't need to and needn't are possible
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: Both don't need to and needn't are possible
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Both forms can express the same meaning here.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 19.1.2 Nobody ___ ever know what happened here. (formal)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Formal use of modal 'need' with negative word 'never' → 'need never'.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 19.1.3 The changes ___ only be minor to make the system work better.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Formal written use of modal 'need' with 'only' → 'need only be'.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 19.1.4 I was nervous before the presentation, but I ___ worried. It went really well.
options: needn't have (= I did worry but it was unnecessary)
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: needn't have (= I did worry but it was unnecessary)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: I DID worry but it was unnecessary → needn't have worried.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 19.1.5 Do you need to / Need you leave so soon? I was hoping you could stay for dinner.
options: Both 'Do you need to' and 'Need you' are possible (need you = more formal/rare)
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: Both 'Do you need to' and 'Need you' are possible (need you = more formal/rare)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: 'Do you need to' is more common; 'Need you' is modal, formal — both possible.
